content.full_text:
Memo from Perryman 90001
"Suggestions for improving
FTB audits"

October 21, 1977

Harlin J. Perryman
777 North First Street
Suite 600
San Jose, California 95112

Dear Mr. Perryman:

Thank you for your recent letter in which you dis-
cussed our hearing on the Franchise Tax Board audits.
As you may have heard, this hearing was postponed and
rescheduled for November 3, 1977.

Since your letter makes several concrete sugges-
tions on how to not only improve the audits but also
the Political Reform Act, I would hope that you would
accept our invitation to address the Commission at the
November meeting.

In any case, I will forward your letter to the
Commissioners.

Thank you for taking the time to express your
views and I look forward to hearing from you whether
you can attend our meeting.

Sincerely,

Robert M. Stern
General Counsel

FMS:nc

LAW OFFICES
OF

HARLIN J. PERRYMAN

777 NORTH FIRST STREET
SUITE 600
SAN JOSE, CALIFORNIA 95112
TELEPHONE: 293-3193 (408)

5268 Scotts Valley Dr.
SCOTTS VALLEY, CALIFORNIA 95066
TELEPHONE: 438-3966 (408)

Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 Kay Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Attention: Robert Stern

Gentlemen:

I have read in your bulletin notice that the FPPC intends to
hold a hearing concerning the auditing procedures of the Franchise
Tax Board re lobbyists and campaign committees. It seems peculiar
that such hearing would be called in view of the FPPC's role
in the Governor's striking of the control language in the
appropriation bill for the FTB which dealt with audits. However,
as a campaign treasurer who has been audited twice, I wish to
submit my comments because I do not relish completing another
questionably effective questionnaire which deals with the problems of the political reform act.

First, I wish to address myself to the "confirmation letters"
and their effect on both contributors and treasurers. When the
letters are sent out concerning our campaign committee, my law
office is deluged with phone calls regarding these letters. The
majority of calls request that I give them the data necessary to
fill out the form because they are not sure of how many fund
raising functions they have attended or when. In order to avoid
alienating a contributor, I have been informing them as to what
my records show, and as tactfully as possible, I explain that
their conduct is of worthless effect because the FTB is checking
on me and they want independent information. A minority of
callers are irate about the inquiry and ask if they can throw
the letter in the waste-basket and they are tired of furnishing
anything to a job-making bureaucracy or something to that effect.

The confirmation letters present another problem with small
contributors because of their problem in determining who is the
donor. For example, donor buys a ticket to a dinner for $50.00
paying from his personal account. He is the owner of a small
business which contributes $50.00 to the campaign. Donor's wife
then executes a check for $50.00 for another fund raiser which
is drawn on the couple's joint bank account. Donor is frequently

of the opinion that he has contributed $150.00 personally instead
of the $50.00 and will report thusly unless it is explained to
him.

In no case, has anyone which your entity has labelled the
"special interests" contacted me concerning confirmation letters.
They obviously understand the law and its procedures. Calls to
me originate with the element of contributors which has barely
broken the reporting threshold.

I oppose the sending of any questionnaire to persons who have
been audited as a waste of time and money. I have completed one
questionnaire which I considered to be a waste of time because there
was no way in it to address the real limitations of the Political
Reform Act. In many cases the right questions did not appear and
when relevant ones did there was a lack of space to be specific.
It seemed to me that the effect of the questionnaire was to
demonstrate what a fine job the bureaucratic agencies were doing.
In some cases, certain departments are doing a good job, but I
for one would like to explain how and why.

I have no complaint about the types of violations listed in
the audit reports, probably because of since the time that I
became treasurer there have been none. The FTB corrected its
earlier policy regarding lobbyists' reports eliminating its
nit'picking policy. The principal problem here seems to me to
be in the enforcement policy. I note in the Andersen & Company
report to the Joint Legislative Audit Committee that the Commission
has not completed investigation of 31 of the 32 audit reports
which indicated substantial noncompliance. I presume that these
principally involved mandatory audits. If your agency is interested
in a real mess, perhaps you should randomly check the reports of
local candidates. Out of curiosity I have checked several of
these reports. A considerable number indicate substantial
violations on their face. It seems here that a committee for
a local candidate is really on the honor system. I have frequently
been contacted by anxious treasurers for these committees regarding
reporting problems, and have tried to help them. However, I
realized that they were safe unless an opponent made a complaint
because otherwise they would never undergo a real audit.

I realize that all agencies connected with enforcement will
raise the excuse that the legislature has not provided sufficient
funds for enforcement. Assuming for the sake of argument that
more funds are warranted for enforcement, it seems unlikely that
the legislature would be foolish enough to provide for more funds
for enforcement which would be substantially used against the
member's own campaign committees. They have enough trouble keeping
treasurers themselves.

It seems that a random auditing procedure such as the Legislature
enacted in its control language in the FTB appropriation would be
the answer to many problems. I wish them luck in the litigation
concerning this. That audit procedure would certainly cut down

-2-

on the time that a treasurer has to spend with the auditors each
year. I have undergone two audits which required me to schedule
several days of time to be available to answer the questions of
the auditors. After a short time both auditors, I believe realized
that they were not going to find anything that was not adequately
documented, but nevertheless because of the existing procedures,
they had to audit laboriously from A to Z my bulky committee
records. If the Internal Revenue Service audited each and every
return filed, the tax system in this country would be wrecked.

I oppose vehemently any extension of the two-year statute
of limitations. First it is already twice as long as that for
most misdemeanors under California law, but even more seriously,
prosecutions would be against people who are largely volunteers
and amateur in quality of inadvertent violations. I can readily
foresee that any flurry of enforcement activity on your part would
largely effect campaign treasurers. The treasurer's control over
a campaign apparatus is limited in any case. He is held responsible
for the financial activities of a motley army of transient and
transient volunteers who know little about the Political Reform
Act and in some cases care less. It is apparent that the drafters
of the act did not understand how political committees function
or they would not have applied draconian standards to such
non-business like entities. It seems only fair that a treasurer
or campaign worker should be able to rest in peace two years
after quiting politics.

I have attempted above only to address my comments to the
matters coming up in your hearing. However, I wish to make this
comment on the reform act generally. In a very substantial number
of instances, it has acted against the very people that it was
intended to help. It is an incumbents' act pure and simple in
its results. It has discouraged amateurs and volunteers because
of fear. It is extremely expensive and worrisome to any new
candidate who desires to run for anything. I hope that any
amendments to improve the act come from someone besides the
drafters of the act and its sponsors. They have adequately
demonstrated to me that they do not understand the political
process.

Sincerely yours,

Harlin J. Perryman

HJP/j

sections.question_synthetic: The document is not a question-answer format; rather it is a letter from Harlan J. Perryman, a campaign treasurer, submitting comments to the FPPC regarding the Franchise Tax Board's auditing procedures and the effectiveness of the Political Reform Act.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document is OCR-scanned incoming correspondence, not an FPPC advice letter. Contains no Q/C sections. The first page is a brief acknowledgment letter from Robert Stein (FPPC General Counsel) dated October 21, 1977, forwarding Perryman's substantive letter to the Commission. The main document is Perryman's detailed comments on audit procedures and political reform compliance. Some OCR artifacts present but content generally legible.

embedding.summary: This is an incoming correspondence from campaign treasurer Harlan J. Perryman to the FPPC expressing concerns about FTB auditing procedures, confirmation letter requirements, audit questionnaires, and the enforcement of the Political Reform Act. He advocates for random auditing procedures and opposes extending the statute of limitations.
